prompt: Translate the following English sentence into French, Japanese, and Arabic, and add a brief note on any phrase that was tricky to render faithfully: "The silence between us said more than any argument could have."
response:
French  
« Le silence entre nous en disait plus que n’aurait pu le faire aucune dispute. »  
Note: The verb phrase « en dire plus que » is the idiomatic way to keep the English idea of “say more,” and the subjunctive « n’aurait pu le faire » is needed to preserve the counter-factual nuance of “could have.”

Japanese  
私たちの間の沈黙は、どんな口論よりも多くを語っていた。  
Note: どんな口論よりも多くを語っていた literally means “was saying more than any argument,” capturing the comparative nuance; Japanese normally omits the explicit “could have,” letting the past tense 語っていた carry the counter-factual feel.

Arabic  
الصمتُ بيننا قال أكثر ممّا كان يستطيع أيّ جدال أن يقوله.  
Note: The structure « أكثر ممّا كان يستطيع … أن يقوله » (“more than any argument was able to say”) is a faithful but compact way to render “could have,” since Arabic lacks a one-word equivalent for that English modal past perfect.
